props.caption: Compute is now power, sited and secured

items.note: Sanctions relief is already moving while the inspection fight hardens: OFAC's temporary license clears Iranian crude and petrochemical sales through 21 August, the IAEA insists inspections will go ahead under the 60-day MOU clock, and Iran has barred current access to the bombed Fordow, Natanz and Isfahan sites except under a final deal with sanctions lifted — so relief arrived in the domain Washington controls while verification became the open fight.

The strait trades like a managed reopening rather than a closing gate: Oman opened temporary corridors with grouped departures and no tolls, roughly 20 million barrels cleared in 24 hours, and Brent slid to around $73.65 — its weakest since 27 February — even as CENTCOM's 55 merchant transits sit at only about 40% of the pre-war daily norm, so the war premium has left the barrel before the old traffic pattern is fully back.

The under-watched Pacific flank is now in public view: Britain, France and Germany flagged novel Chinese activity in the waters east of Taiwan as a threat to regional stability and the safety of international shipping, China's coast guard ran a 'special maritime traffic law-enforcement operation' off the east coast, and Taipei's defence ministry says the warning time is shortening while TSMC anchors the island to the global AI supply chain.

El Obeid remains inside the prevention window — reinforcement, encirclement and about ten consecutive days of RSF drone strikes, with no verified urban ground entry — as the Security Council demanded the RSF halt an imminent assault on a city the Human Rights Council says puts roughly 500,000 civilians at risk; the missing fall-of-the-city confirmation is a limit of wartime evidence, not reassurance, and the protective action still lags the explicit warning.

items.what: The contested Wishah case sharpened rather than resolved: Israel made its evidence public through a military ID to support its claim that the killed Al Jazeera journalist was a Hamas operative, while Al Jazeera maintains he was a targeted journalist — a named casualty whose status the two accounts still dispute.

Russia is shipping crude at a near-record pace even as Kyiv's deep strikes hit refineries and a Crimea rail bridge — the long-range campaign reaching the export and supply chain behind the front while seaborne flows hold up.

No independent evaluation of Sakana's Fugu Ultra has appeared and Anthropic has not named the Qwable distill, while the money moves regardless: Qualcomm bought Modular for about $4bn and SK Hynix is raising up to $29.4bn for AI memory — capital outrunning the verification that would let anyone price the frontier claims.

Brent fell to around $73.65, its weakest since February, as Hormuz fully reopened and stranded cargoes were released — the screen pricing returning barrels and a draining war-risk premium rather than a verdict that the route is settled.
The dollar hit a 13-month high on rate-hike bets and a stock rout, the haven bid and policy repricing pulling capital toward cash even as the oil-shock fear that would normally drive it has unwound.
